text: An API, or Application Programming Interface, allows different software applications to communicate with each other. When working with an API, you typically send HTTP requests, such as GET to retrieve data or POST to send data. The response usually comes in JSON format, which is easy for computers to read and parse. Many APIs require authentication, often using an API key, to ensure only authorized users can access the data. You also need to be aware of rate limits, which restrict how many requests you can make in a certain time period. Finally, good error handling is essential, as you should always check for HTTP status codes like 404 or 500 to handle failures gracefully.
